segments.s713-g97: بدو is the prepositional/clitic combination meaning 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb; گفت is the past tense verb 'said' with خاتون (the subject) following in inverted, poetic word order typical of classical Persian: 'to him said the lady'. کز introduces a relative clause 'who/that', and ایدر نه دور literally 'not far from here' (ایدر = 'here', دور = 'far').
segments.s713-g98: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' (introducing existence), merged with مرغزارست where مرغزار + است ('is a meadow') is compressed into one unit (مرغزارست) — a common poetic contraction; زیبای سور is a genitival/attributive phrase 'beautiful of/for the feast' = 'a beautiful (place) for a feast'. The overall clause states existence: 'there is a certain meadow, a beautiful feast-place.'
segments.s713-g99: جوانان is the plural noun 'young people/young men' (subject). چین here names the land/people 'Chin' (the realm of China) and follows جوانان in a genitive relation ('the young men of Chin'); اندرَان is an archaic/poetic locative 'in/among them' (i.e., in that place), followed by مرغزار 'meadow' as the locative target.
segments.s713-g100: یکی again functions as an indefinite 'a certain' (here introducing the event), جشن is the noun 'festival/celebration', and سازند is the 3rd-person-plural present form 'they make/hold' (classical/persian present), so 'they hold a festival'. گاه بهار is a temporal phrase 'at the time/season of spring' (گاه = season here, بهار = spring).
segments.s713-g101: ازان بیشه is a locative/ablative phrase 'from that grove' (ازان = 'from that', بیشه = 'grove'). پرتاب is a deverbal noun 'a throwing/launch' and combines with یک تیروار to describe the nature of the shot: تیروار (here) is used adverbially/qualitatively as 'like an arrow' or 'in one arrow-like shot' — producing 'an arrow-like launch.' The whole segment conveys 'from that grove an arrow-like shot was hurled.'
segments.s713-g102: یکی is again the indefinite 'one/a certain'. کوه is 'mountain'. بینی here is the archaic 2nd/3rd-person sight-verb form 'you/he see(s)' (poetic; Persian bíni can mean 'you see' or 'one sees') used parenthetically to present what one perceives. سیه‌تر ز قار is a comparative 'blacker than qar' (سیه‌تر = 'darker/blacker', ز = 'than/from').
segments.s713-g103: بران is a prepositional/adverbial particle 'upon that' or 'on it/thereon'. کوه خارا is a noun phrase 'a flinty/hard mountain' (خارا used adjectivally 'flinty, hard'). یکی اژدهاست uses the indefinite یکی + copula-stem اژدهاست ('there is a dragon') — the existential 'there is a dragon' on that mountain; note the poetic fusion of the copula -است as a suffix.
segments.s713-g104: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'. این کشور چین is 'this country/realm of Chin' (این = 'this', کشور = 'country/realm', چین = 'Chin/China'). ازو is an oblique pronoun 'from him/it' referring to the dragon, در is the preposition 'in' and بلاست is a predicative adjective/verb phrase 'is in calamity' (بلا = calamity; here the phrase says the realm is under/within calamity because of it).
segments.s713-g89: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. خاتون is the subject 'the lady'. پس پرده is an adverbial phrase 'behind the curtain' (پس = 'after/behind' placed before پرده 'curtain'). آوا is the object 'a sound/voice' and شنید is the main verb in simple past 'heard' (3rd singular). The clause follows Persian SOV order inside the temporal frame set by چو.
segments.s713-g90: بشد is the past/inchoative of شدن here meaning 'became/grew' (it happened that she became). تیز is a predicate adjective 'alert/quick/keen'. و links the two coordinated actions. بهرام یل را بدید is a full verb phrase: بدید (past of دیدن) 'saw' completes the clause, with یل 'hero' as apposition to the name بهرام and را marking the definite object. The subject ('she', the خاتون) remains understood from the previous clause.
segments.s713-g91: فراوانش combines فراوان 'plentifully' with the enclitic -ش (object pronoun) to mean 'praised her greatly'. بستود is the past of ستودن 'praised'. وکرد is the compound 'and did/made' used to introduce the exclamation that follows, and آفرین is the exclamation 'bravo' or 'well done'.
segments.s713-g92: که introduces the content of the wish ('that ...'). آباد بادا is an optative formula: آباد 'prosperous' + بادا ('may it be') = 'may [it] be prosperous'. بتو is the dative 'to you'. ترک و چین names the two entities wished prosperity (Turk and China) joined by و. Word order places the optative verb after the predicate adjective in this poetic register.
segments.s713-g93: یکی آرزو is 'one wish' (یکی = 'one/a single'). خواهم is the first-person present/future auxiliary from خواستن ('I will/wish to'), here 'I will ask/request'. از شهریار marks the source 'from the king' with the preposition از following the verb phrase (typical Persian ordering: object + verb + source).
segments.s713-g94: که introduces the subordinate desiderative/result clause. باشد is the subjunctive/optative 3rd-person form 'may it be' (here 'may it be so'). بران is a contracted/dialectal form of بر آن 'for/unto that' ('by that' or 'thereby'). آرزو کامگار is a nominal phrase meaning 'wish‑fulfilled/successful' (آرزو 'wish' modified by کامگار 'successful'), so the clause means 'that by that wish I may be successful / that the wish may be fulfilled.'
segments.s713-g95: بدو گفت is the common inverted order 'to her/him said' (بدو = 'to her/him', گفت = 'said'), where the indirect object precedes the verb. بهرام is the speaker (proper name). فرمان تو راست is a predicative clause: فرمان 'command' with possessive تو 'your' and راست 'true/right' as predicate adjective — 'your command is right.'
segments.s713-g96: برین is a poetic contraction of بر + این 'on/with this' ('regarding this'). آرزو کام pairs آرزو 'wish' with کام 'fulfillment/satisfaction' (i.e. 'wish‑fulfillment'); و پیمان تو adds 'and your pledge' as a second item. The final راست predicates the whole phrase ('is true/stands'). The sequence is typical poetic word order where the locution 'برین' governs the following nouns.
segments.s713-g81: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). ننگ (noun) means 'shame' and is the condition's subject. باشد is the present/subjunctive third-person of بودن used here to state a hypothetical ('if there is/is to be'). وگر is a coordinating conditional particle ('but/and if' or 'or if') linking this clause to the next. نام من is a noun + independent pronoun possessor ('my name'); Persian often uses the separate pronoun after the noun (rather than a suffix) in elevated/poetic diction.
segments.s713-g82: بگویم is first-person singular present/subjunctive of گفتن used here in the sense 'were I to say / if I say' and links back to the conditional introduced by اگر/وگر. برآید is a present/subjunctive/optative third-person meaning 'come to pass / be realized' and forms the idiom کام برآید ('a desire becomes fulfilled'). مگر functions as a small rhetorical/interrogative particle here ('perhaps / would it be that...?') that frames the following noun as the thing in question. کام من = 'my desire/its fulfillment,' with من again as the independent possessor.
segments.s713-g83: برآمد is a simple past ('arose/occurred'). برین is the fused poetic form of بر این ('on/at this'), here functioning as a temporal/discourse pointer ('this/that'). نیز means 'also/too'. روز دراز is noun + adjective ('a long day'); Persian allows the adjective after the noun, and the whole phrase is the temporal subject/complement of برآمد.
segments.s713-g84: نهانی is an adverb/adjectival form meaning 'secretly/hidden.' ز is the poetic/prepositional form of از 'from.' هرکس ('every person / everyone') is the object of ز. همی‌داشت is the imperfect/past-continuous form (همی- prefix marks continuous/aspectual) of داشتن 'to have/hold', here 'kept/held.' راز is the thing held—'secret'—and appears at the clause end as object, typical of Persian SOV order (object before verb).
segments.s713-g85: چنان ... که is a common construction meaning 'so ... that / it so happened that.' بد here functions adverbially in the idiom ('in such a way / so'), and که introduces the following clause. خاقان is the subject ('the Khaghan'), یکی is an indefinite 'one / a certain', سور is the direct object 'feast/banquet', and کرد is the past tense verb 'did/held' — overall: 'it was such that the Khaghan held a certain feast.'
segments.s713-g86: جهان را uses را as the definite object marker ('the world' as object). بران is the fused form of بر آن ('on/at that'), locating سور ('that feast') — here 'at that feast.' پر نور is a compound where پر ('full of') modifies نور ('light'), meaning 'full of light / radiant.' کرد is the past verb 'made/caused,' so the clause means 'he made the world full of light at that feast.'
segments.s713-g87: فرستاد is the past tense 'sent.' بهرام یل is a name plus an epithet/appositive ('Bahram, the hero'). The object marker را is not written separately here but appears attached in the following verb (رابخواند); رابخواند is a single written unit meaning 'called/summoned him' (past tense), so the sequence expresses 'he sent (someone) and summoned Bahram the hero.'
segments.s713-g88: چو is a conjunction meaning 'when.' آمدش is آمد + enclitic -ش ('he came' / 'came to him' depending on context); here it marks 'he came.' برتخت is the fused prepositional phrase بر تخت ('onto the throne'), with زرین ('golden') as its adjective. نشاند is an archaic/transitive past verb meaning 'caused to sit / seated' (causative force: 'set him on the throne'). Word order places the locative phrase before the verb, then the past verb completes the action.
segments.s713-g121: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. The sequence چو + از دور (adverbial 'from afar') + بینند (present-form used narratively: 'they see' / 'they saw') introduces the clause; چنگال اوی is the object where اوی is the enclitic possessive 'his' attached to چنگال. Ferdowsi uses the present form بینند for past narrative.
segments.s713-g122: برو here functions as a verb/verb-like action 'they (then) went/turned (toward, rushed at)' with an implied plural subject carried over from the previous clause. The list پشت و گوش و سر و یال names the targets and is coordinated by و; the final اوی ('his') governs the whole series (i.e. 'his back and ear and head and mane').
segments.s713-g123: بغرد is an intransitive onomatopoetic/present-form verb meaning 'it utters a cry/roars' (used here in the narrative present). بدرد is 'to (cause) pain' or 'to pierce', and دل مرد جنگ is a genitive chain 'the heart of the man of war' (مردِ جنگ = 'warrior'). The verb acts on the warrior's heart: 'its cry pierces the warrior's heart.'
segments.s713-g124: مر is an archaic form meaning 'mرا / to me / as for me' (a topical dative/emphatic), followed by او را 'him' with the object marker; چه ... و چه ... is the correlative 'whether ... or ...' listing شیر (lion), پیل (elephant) and نهنگ (whale/huge monster). The clause reads as a topical remark about the speaker's attitude toward that creature: 'as for me — whether lion or elephant or whale.'
segments.s713-g125: کس 'person/anyone' here functions in the sense 'no one' given the negative/deterring context; اندر 'in/among' is a locative particle. نیارد شدن is an older/poetic verbal idiom meaning 'to come forward / to venture (before someone)'; شدن is the auxiliary/verb 'to be' here. پیش اوی means 'in his presence / before him' (اوی = 'his'). Altogether: 'no one ventured to come before him.'
segments.s713-g126: چوگیرد is a temporal/subordinating particle 'when / whenever' (introducing a conditional/occurrent clause). شمار 'number' is the subject; کم و بیش is the paired expression 'less and more' = 'few or many'; اوی 'his' is the possessive referring back to the person/force in question. The clause means 'when his numbers were few or many' (i.e. regardless of size of his force).
segments.s713-g127: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (i.e. 'he said to him'); گفت here is the reporting verb 'said' (Ferdowsi uses گفت in the narrative sense 'he said to him'). بهرام is the speaker's name and is the grammatical subject; فردا پگاه is an adverbial time phrase 'tomorrow at dawn' placed after the name.
segments.s713-g128: بیایم is a first-person subjunctive/potential 'I will come / let me come' used to report Bahram's intention; ببینم is a subordinate subjunctive 'I may see / I will see' expressing purpose or result (بیایم ببینم = 'I will come to see'). من is emphatic 'I'; این جشنگاه is the direct object 'this place/occasion of festivity.'
segments.s713-g105: یکی = “a certain / one” (indefinite subject). شیر = “lion” (noun, placed before what follows as a compound/name). خواند here is the past (poetic/older) form of خواندن used in the sense “to call (a thing by a name)” rather than “to read”; همی is the old continuous/emphatic particle (here: iterative/continuous nuance, often left untranslated: “would call” or “kept calling”). The segment literally says “one (person) called (it) ‘lion Kapish’.” Note: کپیش is an unusual/archaic form in this line (see senses).
segments.s713-g106: دگر = “another / other” (refers to a different person). نیز = “also / likewise.” نامش = نام + -ش (name + third-person possessive clitic) = “its / his name.” نداند is the older (poetic) negative/present-past form of دانستن meaning “does not know / did not know” (here: ‘did not know’). همی again is the emphatic/continuous particle; together the clause says “another likewise did not know its name.”
segments.s713-g107: یکی = “a certain / one.” دخترم = دختر + -م “my daughter” (possessive clitic). بد ز here is a compressed/poetic sequence: ز = “from / of,” خاقان = “the khagan / ruler,” چین = “China.” The intended sense is: “one daughter of mine (was) from/of the Khagan of China” (poetic economy omits an explicit verb or uses بد as an archaic element—see senses note).
segments.s713-g108: که = relative “who / whom / that.” خورشید here is used metaphorically “the sun” (= splendid/bright, i.e. radiant beauty). کردی is the 2nd-person past/perfect “you made / you did” (so خورشید کردی = “you made (her) like the sun” or “you likened her to the sun”). برو آفرین functions as an exclamatory phrase meaning roughly “bravo!” or “well done / praise!” — in older Persian برو + آفرین is an idiomatic applause. The clause thus praises the addressee’s comparison or praise of the maiden’s radiance.
segments.s713-g109: از = “from.” ایوان = “iwan / portico / veranda.” بشد is an older/poetic past/inchoative form of شدن; in context it functions as a past motion verb meaning “he came / went” (here: “he came / stepped down”). نزد = “to / near / in the presence of” (so بشد نزد = “came to / drew near”). آن جشنگاه = “that place of celebration / festival-place.” Word order (verb before adjunct) is typical of the epic/poetic register.
segments.s713-g110: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause “for / that / because.” خاقان = “the khagan (ruler).” به نخچیر = a fixed phrase meaning “to the hunt / for hunting” (نخچیر = game / hunting). بد in this construction is an archaic/uncertain element (poetic auxiliary or adverb) — the sense here is best rendered as part of the past narration (“had gone” / “was gone”) or an intensifier; with با سپاه (“with an army”) the clause means “the Khagan was out hunting with his troops.” با = “with,” سپاه = “army / host.”
segments.s713-g111: بیامد = past tense “came” (simple past, narrative). ز = “from.” کوه = “mountain.” اژدهای = “dragon” (ezāfe-like form used in older epic diction) and دژم = adjective “sullen / grim” modifying the dragon. The segment is a straightforward narrative clause: “A grim dragon came from the mountain.”
segments.s713-g112: کشید is the past of کشیدن; in this context it carries the sense “drew / removed / took away / ruined” (poetic use: to seize or spoil). آن بهار = “that spring” (season or metaphorical spring). م + را in the line are the split pieces of مرا = “me” (object clitic) + object marker را in the manuscript’s segmentation; together they indicate that آن بهار is taken away from the narrator: “he took that spring from me.” او = “he” (the dragon). بدم = a possessive-qualifying nominal (literally “my bad/ill”) here meaning “my bane / my ruin.” The two clauses therefore mean: “He took that spring from me; he was my doom.” Note: the manuscript splits (م + را) are editorial/poetic and correspond to the object “mara / me.”
segments.s713-g129: به + noun marks an instrument or source ('by/through'); نیروی یزدان = 'the power of God' (poetic word order, ezāfe relation is implied rather than explicitly marked). که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; او داد زور is a past action 'he gave strength/might' with داد (gave) as the finite verb and او as subject. Note the poetic inversion: داد (verb) comes immediately before زور (its object) instead of the more common او زور داد.
segments.s713-g130: This is an appositive/title describing the same divine referent: بلند (adjective) precedes آفریننده 'creator' to yield 'lofty creator'. ماه وهور lists the created things ('the moon and Vahūr/sun'); in poetic Persian the ezāfe is often omitted and the two nouns are juxtaposed as a single phrase.
segments.s713-g131: بپردازم is a 1st‑person singular form built with the verbal prefix ب- plus the present/subjunctive stem; in epic Persian it functions as a future/intentional 'I will (do) / I shall take'. از + اژدها marks the source/agent 'from the dragon'. جشنگاه is the direct object 'festival-place' (the object marker را is omitted here for poetic compactness).
segments.s713-g132: چو is a conjunction meaning 'when' (introducing a temporal clause). شبگیر functions as a collective subject 'those who come by night / night‑visitors' (an older/poetic noun). ما را uses the object pronoun with the postposed object marker را ('us' as definite object). نمایند is 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive ('they show'); راه is the thing shown 'the way'. The clause order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: 'they show us the way' appears as 'شبگیر ما را نمایند راه'.
segments.s713-g73: همی is an older emphatic/progressive particle here (literary), placed before the clause; شیر کپی خورد is a simple SOV clause: شیر (lion) is subject, کپی the object/name of the beast, and خورد is the past 3rd‑singular verb ‘ate/struck/befell’. دخترم (daughter‑am) is the phrase that follows as the person affected, with the possessive suffix -م marking “my daughter.” Together the segment asserts that the lion and the named beast are involved with the daughter (past event).
segments.s713-g74: بگوییم is a 1st‑person‑plural hortative/subjunctive/optative form (literary) meaning ‘we/they may say’ or ‘it will be said’; here it introduces reported speech. و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’. ننگی is a noun/adjective meaning ‘shame, disgrace’ and شود is the 3rd‑singular present/subjunctive auxiliary ‘becomes / will be’ so ننگی شود = ‘becomes a disgrace’. گوهرم is ‘my gem’ with the possessive suffix -م used metaphorically for ‘my honour’ or ‘my jewel.’ The whole segment states the consequence: if they say it, my honour will be shamed.
segments.s713-g75: ندانند is the 3rd‑person‑plural negative present (literary) ‘they do not know’. کان here is a literary/deictic particle meaning ‘that’ (introducing the content clause). اژدهای دژم is a noun + adjectival epithet: اژدهای ‘dragon’ and دژم ‘grim/sullen’ modifying it. Together this segment forms the subject of the following verb‑clause: ‘they do not know that the grim dragon...’
segments.s713-g76: همی again marks a continuous or emphatic action. کوه آهن is a genitive/compound meaning ‘a mountain of iron’ (کوه + آهن). رباید is a literary 3rd‑person form of ربودن here meaning ‘seizes / carries off’ (present/habitual force in epic style). به دم literally ‘with (its) breath’ uses به + دم; دم is ‘breath’ (here the dragon’s breath). The clause thus states the mighty action the dragon is capable of.
segments.s713-g77: اگر introduces a conditional: ‘if’. دختر شاه is a genitive phrase ‘the king’s daughter’ (noun + possessor). نامی بود is ‘was famed / would be renowned’ — نامی is an adjective ‘famous’ and بود is the past/copular verb; in classical Persian the past often conveys a subjunctive or counterfactual conditional sense: ‘if she were famous…’.
segments.s713-g78: همان ‘that/same’ focuses/points to the king just mentioned; شاه را uses the definite‑object/topic marker را to topicalize ‘the king’. جان گرامی بود is a nominal predicate: جان ‘life/soul’ + گرامی ‘dear, precious’ + بود ‘was’. The whole means ‘that very king’s life would be precious (to him)’, i.e. his life/ honour would be valued in that situation.
segments.s713-g79: بدو گفت خاتون is a basic ditransitive reporting clause: بدو ‘to him’ (prepositional/old dative), گفت ‘said’ (past third person), خاتون ‘the lady’ (subject following the verb in epic ordering). که introduces the reported content: من کین خویش = ‘I (من) [will take] my vengeance’; کین ‘vengeance’ is the object and خوی + ش is the possessive construction (خوی ‘own/kin/disposition’ + suffix ش ‘his/her/its’ — together here functioning as the reflexive/possessive ‘my own’ when attached to من in the clause).
segments.s713-g80: بخواهم is 1st‑person‑singular subjunctive/optative ‘I will seek/wish/take’ (literary). ز بهر is the fixed poetic prepositional phrase ‘for/on behalf of/for the sake of’ (ز = from/because of, بهر = for). جهان‌بین is an epithet or compound noun ‘world‑seer’ / ‘worldly‑wise’ and here likely refers to the person in relation to whom she seeks vengeance; خوی + ش again marks the possessive (‘my own’). The segment thus completes her declaration of intent: she will seek vengeance for her (own) world‑seer/kinsman.
segments.s713-g113: کنون (temporal adverb) sets the time 'now/presently'. هر بهاری is a temporal noun phrase 'every spring' with the adjective-like بهاری after هر. بران مرغزار is a locative phrase: بران = بر + آن ('on/to that'), placed before مرغزار ('meadow') in an archaic/poetic word order to mean 'to/on that meadow'.
segments.s713-g114: چنان هم بیاید is a comment on manner: چنان 'thus/in the same way' + هم 'likewise', and بیاید is a 3rd-person present/hortative form used narratively ('it comes' / 'it may come'). ز بهر شکار is a purpose phrase: ز (here 'because/for') + بهر ('for, for the sake of') followed by شکار ('hunt, prey'), so together 'for hunting' or 'for the sake of prey'.
segments.s713-g115: برین شهر = بر این شهر ('in/at this city') in an older orthography. ما را functions as a dative/experiencer ('for us' / 'of us')—this is an archaic order meaning 'we had no ...'. جوانی is the noun 'youth' (what was lacking) and نماند is the past-tense negative 'did not remain / was not left'.
segments.s713-g116: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' pointing back; نامور is an adjective 'renowned'; پهلوانی here denotes the heroic person/heroism (poetic nominal use) and functions as the noun that نماند ('did not remain') refers to. The clause repeats the verbless/nominal pattern of the previous line with نماند as the main verbal predicate.
segments.s713-g117: شدند is the past 3rd‑person plural of شدن ('they became/were'), here functioning with the predicate noun هلاک to yield 'they were destroyed/they perished'. از پی = 'in pursuit of/after' (a fixed prepositional phrase). شیرکپی is a compound noun (here 'young lion' / lion‑cub) and هلاک is a predicate noun/adjective meaning 'destroyed/ruined'.
segments.s713-g118: برانگیخت is a past form 'was/was raised' (here passive/impersonal 'rose/was stirred up'). از بوم آباد خاک is a source phrase with poetic word order: از 'from', بوم آباد 'the inhabited/prosperous homeland', and خاک 'dust/soil' as the thing that was raised—literally 'the dust from the prosperous land was raised'.
segments.s713-g119: سواران چینی = 'Chinese horsemen/riders' (noun plus adjective). و links to مردان کار, a genitival/attributive phrase 'men of action/deeds' (مردان = men, کار qualifying them as 'men of deeds' = valiant or skilled men). The phrase groups two kinds of fighters.
segments.s713-g120: بسی is an adverb meaning 'much/in great numbers' (poetic usage). تاختند is past 3rd‑person plural of تاختن 'they charged/rode swiftly'. اندران = اندر + آن ('in/within that') and precedes کوهسار ('mountain-tract') to yield 'into/within that mountain region'—a typical inverted/poetic locative order.
segments.s713-g57: بدو گفت خاتون که با فر اوی is ordered as 'to him said the lady that with his farr...' — بدو (to him) is an indirect-object prepositional phrase placed before the verb گفت (said). که introduces the subordinate clause; با فر اوی literally 'with his farr' (با + noun + possessive اوی), where اوی is the possessive 'his/her' attached after فر.
segments.s713-g58: سزد‌گر بنازیم در پر اوی uses سزد‌گر (lit. 'fitting' / 'appropriate') plus a 1st-person-plural present/subjunctive بنازیم 'we should boast / be proud' (the verb form expresses what is fitting to do). در پر اوی is a locative/figurative phrase 'in his feather(s)' (پر = feather/plumage) functioning as the domain of the pride; اوی again is the possessive 'his'.
segments.s713-g59: یکی آرزو زو بخواهم درست: یکی آرزو 'one desire/one request' is the object; زو (here written as ز + و) = 'from him' (poetic contraction of از او). بخواهم is 1st-person present/subjunctive 'I will ask/seek' or 'I ask of him'; درست is an adverb/adjective 'proper/indeed' modifying the request.
segments.s713-g60: چو خاقان نگردد بدان کارسست: چو here = 'if' (conditional). خاقان is the subject 'the khāqān / supreme ruler.' نگردد is negative present/subjunctive of گشتن 'become/turn/attend to' (i.e. 'does not turn/attend to it'). بد( + )ان functions as 'to that / for that' (ب = prefix 'to/into' plus دان forming بدان), and کارسست is a predicative adjective 'ineffective / unfit for the task', yielding 'if the khāqān does not take it on, he is unfit for that work.'
segments.s713-g61: بخواهد مگر ز اژدها کین من:بخواهد is 3rd-person present/subjunctive 'he may wish' or 'he will want.' مگر here serves to introduce a supposition ('perhaps' / 'unless') and z (ز) + object mean 'from the dragon' (ز اژدها). کین من = 'vengeance/hatred concerning me' (کین + possessive من), i.e. 'vengeance against me.'
segments.s713-g62: برو بشنود درد و نفرین من stacks two imperatives/subjunctival actions: برو 'go' (imperative), and بشنود is 3rd-person subjunctive of شنیدن 'that he may hear' (purpose/result). درد و نفرین من = 'my pain and my curse' (درد = emotional/afflictive sorrow here; نفرین = curse), the direct objects of بشنود.
segments.s713-g63: بدو گفت کهتر‌گر این داستان is structured 'to him said (that) kehtergar this tale' — بدو 'to him', گفت 'said', کهتر‌گر is a vocative or address 'O belittler / detractor' (one who would belittle), and این داستان 'this tale' is the object/topic being mentioned; the clause sets up the command that follows.
segments.s713-g64: بخواند برو مهتر راستان combines an optative/command and an imperative: بخواند is a 3rd-person subjunctive/optative 'let him read / may he recite', followed by the imperative برو 'go'. مهتر راستان is the destination/recipient 'the chief (مهتر) Rāstān' (a name/title); together: 'let him read it — go to Chief Rāstān.'
segments.s713-g49: 'به ایران' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in/at Iran'; the idiomatic sequence 'یکی چند گه' is a literary time adverb meaning 'once' or 'at one time'; 'شاه بود' is the simple past copula ('there was a king'). Word order is literary (locative + temporal phrase + nominal predicate).
segments.s713-g50: 'سرتاج' (literally 'head‑crown') names the crown; 'او' is the possessive/3rd‑person pronoun 'his'; 'برتر' is the comparative/adjective 'superior/loftier'; 'از ماه' is the standard comparison 'than the moon'; 'بود' is the past copula 'was'. The clause says the crown of that king was higher/ brighter than the moon.
segments.s713-g51: 'بزرگانش' ('his great ones') means 'his nobles/elders'; 'خوانند' is the literary present/plural or generalizing present of 'to call' (here 'call' / 'are wont to call'); 'بهرام گرد' is the personal name and epithet (Bahrām‑Gerd). The verb 'خوانند' links the subject ('his great men') to the name they give.
segments.s713-g52: 'که' introduces a relative clause modifying the name ('who/that'); 'از خسروان' means 'among/from the kings' (partitive/associative use of از); 'نام' here = 'name' in the sense 'reputation'; 'مردی' in this context conveys 'manliness' / 'heroic renown'; 'ببرد' is the literary present/subjunctive/habitual of 'برداشتن/بردن' meaning 'to bear/earn/hold' (here 'to have or win a name'). Together: 'who had a name among the kings.'
segments.s713-g53: 'کنون' = 'now'; 'تا' here introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'until'; 'بیامد' is the literary/possible form 'may come' or 'comes' (dependent on 'تا'); 'ز ایران' = 'from Iran'; 'بچین' is an imperative 'gather/assemble' addressed to addressees. The clause reads as a temporal directive: 'Now, until he comes from Iran, assemble.'
segments.s713-g54: 'بلرزد' is the literary present/epic verb 'it quakes / trembles'; 'همی' is an emphatic/adverbial particle (here 'indeed/just'); the prepositional phrase 'زیر اسپش زمین' places the locus: 'under his steed the earth' (normal English order: 'the earth trembles beneath his steed'). Poetic word order places the locative phrase after the verb.
segments.s713-g55: 'خداوند' here is used as the name/title applied ('Khoda‑vand', lit. 'lord'); 'خواند' again is the literary 'call' ('they call' or 'are wont to call'); 'همی' is an adverbial/emphatic particle; 'مهترش' = 'his chief' (مهتر + possessive suffix). The line states that his chief was called Khoda‑vand.
segments.s713-g56: 'همی' is emphatic; 'تاج شاهی' is a noun phrase 'the crown of kingship' (تاج = crown, شاهی = kingship/regal status); 'نهد' is the literary 3rd‑person singular of 'نهادن' meaning 'to place/set'; 'بر سرش' is the prepositional phrase 'upon his head' (بر = on, سر = head, ش = his). The clause means 'they placed the royal crown on his head.'
segments.s713-g1: چو چندی برآمد برین روزگار is a temporal clause: چو + چندی = “after/when a while (had passed).” برآمد is the past verb “arose/came forth.” برین is the compound of بر + ین (بر + این) meaning “this/that” in poetic spelling, so برین روزگار = “this/that time/era.” Word order is inverted and compressed as typical in poetry.
segments.s713-g2: شب و روز آسایش آموزگار is a verbless nominal clause: “night and day” (شب و روز) is presented with a predicate noun phrase آسایش آموزگار. The copula ('was/served as') is omitted poetically, so the literal reading is “night and day (were) a teacher of ease,” i.e. day and night brought/afforded comfort.
segments.s713-g3: چنان بد که در کوه چین آن زمان: چنان بد که introduces a result/comparison clause “so bad that….” در کوه چین is a locative phrase “in Mount Chin” (proper name), and آن زمان places the action in ‘that time/period.’ The conjunction که links to the following clause that states the consequence.
segments.s713-g4: دد و دام بودی فزون از گمان: دد و دام is a coordinated subject “beasts and snares/traps.” بودی is the past copula (poetic form of بود), and فزون از گمان is an adverbial phrase meaning “more numerous/abundant than imagination (one would think).” The whole clause means they were more plentiful than one would expect.
segments.s713-g5: ددی بود مهتر ز اسپی بتن: ددی is the noun referring to a creature (the exact lexical sense isn't among the provided senses and is therefore left unspecified). بود is the past copula “was.” مهتر here conveys greater/larger (poetic usage) and is compared with ز + اسپی بتن = “than a horse in body” (اسپی = horse, بتن = on the body). The construction expresses physical size: it was larger-bodied than a horse.
segments.s713-g6: فروهشته چون مشک گیسو رسن describes the creature's hair: فروهشته is a past-participial/adjective “fallen/scattered,” and چون introduces a simile “like.” مشک گیسو = “musk-like locks” (i.e. fragrant or dark/handsome tresses) and رسن (rein/rope) is another simile for the hair’s quality or form. Adjectives and similes follow the noun as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s713-g7: به تن زرد و گوش و دهانش سیاه is a descriptive clause using به to introduce the bodily attributes (“with/in body ...”). تن زرد = “a yellow/pale body,” then coordinated parts گوش و دهانش with the possessive enclitic -ش on دهان (his mouth), followed by the adjective سیاه. Adjectives follow the nouns in this order and the enclitic indicates third-person possession.
segments.s713-g8: ندیدی کس او را مگر گرمگاه is a negative existential statement with poetic word order. ندیدی is the negative past of دیدن (“did not see”); کس = “anyone/someone”; او را is the third-person object with the accusative marker را; مگر = “except”; گرمگاه names the exception (“the warm place”/bath or summer abode). Together: “No one saw him except (in) the warm place.”
segments.s713-g25: همان (demonstrative) = 'that/the same', used here to pick out a previously mentioned woman; نیز = 'also/too' (adds her action to prior actions). خاتون is the head noun 'lady/consort'. به + کاخ اندورن marks location/direction: 'in/at the inner palace' (کاخ = palace, اندورن = inner chamber). Word order is typical classical Persian: modifiers and particles precede the head.
segments.s713-g26: همی is the habitual/progressive particle here ('did/was doing' / iterative aspect). رای زد is an idiom: رای = 'counsel/plan' and زدن in this collocation means 'to give/express' a decision or counsel (not literally 'to strike'); با یکی رهنمون = 'with a certain guide' (با = 'with', یکی = 'a certain one/someone', رهنمون = 'guide/guidance'). The verb زد takes the object رای and the accompanying instrumental phrase با ...
segments.s713-g27: چو = 'when' introducing a temporal clause. آن شیر کپی = 'that lion Kapi' (شیر = 'lion'; کپی here is a name/epithet). ز + کوهش = 'from his mountain' (ز is the poetic form of از, and -ش is the possessive 'his'). بدید is the classical past of دیدن 'saw' (the b- prefix is common in epic Persian forms).
segments.s713-g28: فرود آمد is a compound verbal phrase 'came down/descended' (perfective past). او را uses را as the definite direct-object marker ('him'). به دم is instrumental/locative here 'by the tail' (به used to mark the instrument/means). درکشید is 'drew/pulled (to/into)' (کشیدن = 'to pull/draw'); together the phrase means 'dragged him by the tail to the gate/entrance.'
segments.s713-g29: بیک دم is an adverbial expression 'in one breath/at one moment' (an abrupt, single instant). شد = 'became/was' (perfective). او is the subject 'he'. از جهان = 'from the world' (از = 'from/out of'). در نهان = 'into hiding/hidden' (در = 'in', نهان = 'hidden'), so the clause states that he was suddenly gone from the world, hidden.
segments.s713-g30: سرآمد is 'foremost/leader' (predicative or nominal). بران here functions as 'among/on those' (i.e., 'among them'). خوب‌چهره = 'beautiful-faced/handsome' describing the class, and جهان = 'of the world'. Together: 'he was foremost among the world's fair‑faced (people)'.
segments.s713-g31: چو = 'when'. خاقان is the title 'khāqān' (ruler). شنید = 'heard'. آن = 'that' (referring to the news). سیه کرد روی is a light verb construction: سیه = 'black/gloomy' and کرد ('made') turns it into 'made his face dark' i.e. 'grew gloomy/grimaced', with روی = 'face' as the affected noun.
segments.s713-g32: همان = 'that same' (anaphoric). مادرش = 'his mother' (مادر + possessive -ش). نیر here appears in the text where one would expect a particle meaning 'also' (نیز); in the given lemma list its senses don't match the conjunctive use, so it functions as an emphatic/connecting particle 'too' in context. بر کند موی is a verbal sequence: برکند (بر + کند) 'to tear/pull off' and موی = 'hair' — i.e. 'she tore at / pulled out her hair' (an idiomatic expression of grief).
segments.s713-g65: This is a negative declarative clause with the 2nd-person subject 'تو' at the front for emphasis. 'از' introduces the source/thing ('from/of'), and 'شیر کپی' is a compound where 'شیر' (lion) is qualified by 'کپی' (an imitation/copy). 'نیابی' is the negative present/imperative-form of the verb 'یاب' (here functioning as a 2nd-person form meaning 'you will not/find you do not find'), and 'نشان' is the direct object 'sign/trace'. Word order is subject — prepositional phrase — verb — object, typical in Persian poetry.
segments.s713-g66: 'مگر' introduces an exception meaning 'unless'. 'کشته' is a past participle/adjective meaning 'killed' and supplies the condition; there is an asyndetic coordination after it: 'و گرگ پایش کشان' is an absolute participial construction where 'گرگ' is the noun, 'پایش' = 'its paw' (enclitic 'ش' = its), and 'کشان' is the present participle 'dragging'. Together this gives the conditional exception: only if it is killed and the wolf drags its paw will you find a mark.
segments.s713-g67: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause 'when'. Word order is subject then verb: 'خاتون شنید' ('the lady heard'). 'این سخن' is the object 'these words', and 'شاد شد' is a adjectival-predicative clause with 'شد' as the copular/auxiliary meaning 'became' — 'she became glad.'
segments.s713-g68: The preposition 'ز' ('from', here poetic) marks the source or cause: 'ز تیمار' = 'from (her) care/custody'. 'آن دختر آزاد شد' literally 'that girl became free' — 'آزاد' is predicate adjective and 'شد' is the past copula/auxiliary, so the clause means 'the girl was freed / released from care.'
segments.s713-g69: 'همی‌تاخت' is an imperfect/continuous past verbal form meaning 'he rode on/was charging'. 'تا' marks the endpoint 'until', followed by the locative 'پیش خاقان' ('in the presence of / before the khaghan'). 'رسید' is the past tense 'arrived/reached'. The clause expresses continuous action up to the point of arrival.
segments.s713-g70: 'یکایک' is an adverb 'one by one/every single one' modifying the verb 'بگفت' ('he said/told'). 'آنچ' = 'that which/what' is a relative/indefinite object, followed by the past verbs 'دید و شنید' ('saw and heard'), so the sequence means 'he recounted, one by one, what he had seen and heard.'
segments.s713-g71: 'بدو' is the prepositional clitic 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb. 'گفت خاقان' — the agent 'خاقان' follows the verb here (poetic inversion), so the phrase reads 'the khaghan said to him'. The subordinate particle 'که' introduces the content clause 'عاری بود' where 'عاری' = 'free (from blame/reproach)' and 'بود' is the copula 'was'.
segments.s713-g72: 'بجایی که' functions as 'to/into a place where' (poetic usage of بجا), with 'که' introducing the relative clause. 'چون' = 'like/as', so 'چون من سواری بود' = 'there was a horseman like me' (with 'بود' as the past copula). The whole segment qualifies the khaghan's statement: it was free/permissible concerning places where a rider like the speaker was present.
segments.s713-g41: چنان بد که is a poetic contraction of چنان بود که, meaning “it so happened that / such was the case that.” یک روز is the temporal phrase “one day.” دیدش is the past transitive verb دید with the enclitic object -ش (“saw him/her/it”); in this poetic order the verb precedes its subject. سوار is the subject “rider/horseman,” placed after the verb by inversion.
segments.s713-g42: از ایران is a simple prepositional phrase “from Iran.” همان نیز is emphatic/identifying — “that very one / moreover,” and صد نامدار is an elliptical description “(one of the) a hundred renowned (men).” The clause is elliptical: the noun phrase qualifies the rider introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s713-g43: پیاده فراوان is a noun phrase where پیاده means ‘infantry/foot soldiers’ and فراوان qualifies it as ‘many’ or ‘numerous.’ به پیش اندرون is an adverbial/directional cluster: به پیش ‘forward / to the front’ and اندرون ‘into the inner (court/quarters),’ giving the destination for the action expressed by the following verb.
segments.s713-g44: همی‌راند is a habitual/continuous past-form (همی- prefix + past stem) meaning “was driving / was leading.” The subject بهرام follows the verb (poetic inversion). با رهنمون is an instrumental phrase “with a guide,” marking accompaniment or means; the object of راندن (the foot soldiers) is supplied by the previous segment.
segments.s713-g45: بپرسید is the past (reported) sense ‘asked’ here (the narrative past). خاتون is the subject ‘the lady.’ که introduces the indirect question clause. این مرد کیست is the content of that question: این مرد ‘this man’ + کیست, where کی is the interrogative ‘who’ and ست is the copula ‘is’ (present form used in the indirect question).
segments.s713-g46: که again links/explains the reason or description. با برز و با فره sets up two instrumental/possessive qualities: با برز ‘with exaltedness/high rank’ and با فره ‘with farr (divine glory).’ ایزدیست is a descriptive predicate meaning ‘is divine / of the godly’ — together indicating the man possesses exalted, divinely‑bestowed glory.
segments.s713-g47: بدو is the dative/enclitic phrase ‘to him / unto him.’ گفت is the past ‘said’ (introducing direct speech or command). کهتر is a vocative/adjective ‘the lesser / inferior one’ used as address. That introduces the admonition clause: که + دوری ز کام — here دوری is a verbal/nominal injunction ‘keep away / be distant’ and ز کام is the prepositional object ‘from desire,’ so the whole phrase is an imperative/advice: ‘stay away from desire.’
segments.s713-g48: که begins a subordinate/explanatory clause (‘for / that’). بهرام is the proper name subject; یل is an appositive noun ‘hero’ (no copula required). راندانی is an epithet or participial descriptor attached to the hero (here functioning as a name/epithet), and بنام means ‘called / named,’ so the phrase says Bahram is a hero known/ named Rândâni.
segments.s713-g33: ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'because of'; دردش is a noun + enclitic ش 'her' = 'her pain'. همه‌ساله ('yearly') modifies the ensuing verbal phrase. گریان is a present-participial adjective/noun 'weeping', and بدند is an archaic 3rd-person-plural past/habitual auxiliary meaning 'they were' or 'they kept (being)'; together گریان بدند = 'they were/kept weeping'.
segments.s713-g34: چو here introduces a simile ('as'/'as if'). بر آتش تیز is a prepositional phrase 'on/over a sharp/fierce fire' (تیز qualifies آتش). بریان is a participle 'roasted/burned', and the same archaic auxiliary بدند gives 3rd-pl. past/habitual sense; the clause means 'as if they were roasted on a fierce fire.'
segments.s713-g35: همی is a frequentative/adverbial particle meaning 'continually' or 'still'. چاره 'remedy/solution' is the object. جستند is the past plural of جستن here meaning 'they sought' (sense 'to seek'). زان 'from that' or 'for that' points back to the problem, and اژدها is 'dragon' — so the whole segment = 'they kept seeking a remedy for that dragon.'
segments.s713-g36: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that/which' or here marking the embedded question). تا is 'until'. چین in this context is the toponym 'Chin' (China). کی is the interrogative 'when', and آید is the present/subjunctive-like form 'will come'/'come'. ز + چنگش = 'from its grip/claw' (چنگش = چنگ + possessive ش). رها is a predicate adjective/participle 'free'/'released'. The clause asks 'until when will Chin be freed from its grip.'
segments.s713-g37: چو here means 'when'. بهرام is the proper name Bahram. جنگ مقاتوره is a doubled/qualified phrase meaning a fierce fight or combat (جنگ 'battle' + مقاتوره 'combat'). کرد is the simple past 'did/made' — 'when Bahram waged/engaged in the fierce battle.'
segments.s713-g38: وزان is a fused form of و + زان 'and/from that'. مرد جنگی is a compound 'warlike man' or 'warrior' (مرد 'man/hero' + جنگی 'warlike'). برآورد is a past form meaning 'brought forth/produced' (برآوردن), and گرد here is the relic of an old verbal auxiliary/particle (from گردیدن) forming the past narrative stamp; together 'from that he produced/raised a warrior.'
segments.s713-g39: همی‌رفت is an imperfect/habitual past form ('would go' / 'kept going'). خاتون is 'the lady'. بدیدار is a prepositional/infinitival unit 'to visit/to see (meet)'. اوی is the old pronoun referring to the person visited ('him'/'her'/'that one'). Word order places the verb first (poetic), with the object/goal (بدیدار اوی) following.
segments.s713-g40: بهر کس = 'to/for everyone' (بهر = 'to/for', کس = 'anyone/everyone'). همی‌گفت is habitual past 'she kept saying' or 'would tell'. کردار اوی is 'his deeds' (کردار 'deed', اوی the possessive pronoun 'his/her'); the clause means 'to everyone she would tell his deeds.'
segments.s713-g17: A compact, verbless descriptive phrase. 'دو' (two) precedes the noun 'لب' and the adjective 'سرخ' follows the noun (noun + adjective order, common in Persian poetry). The coordinate 'و' links the next nominal phrase 'بینی چو تیغ قلم' where 'چو' introduces a simile ('like/as') and 'تیغ قلم' is a poetic compound meaning 'the point/blade of a pen' (noun+noun).
segments.s713-g18: 'دو' again numerally governs the following phrase (two of something—here understood as the paired facial features). 'بیجاده' is an archaic/poetic epithet placed before the participial/adjectival 'خندان' (adjective after the noun is normal); the coordinating 'و' links the final phrase 'نرگس دژم' in which 'دژم' is a postposed adjective modifying 'نرگس'. The whole line is a list of physical epithets (no main verb).
segments.s713-g19: 'بران' is a locative/contrastive particle meaning 'on/at that' or 'regarding that'; it introduces the following noun phrase 'دخت لرزان' (noun followed by the adjective 'لرزان' = 'trembling'). 'بدی' here functions as an adjectival attribute 'bad/unfortunate' modifying the following 'مام وباب' (colloquial 'mam' = mother; 'وباب' is printed together but is best read as 'و باب' = 'and father' in colloquial speech) — the phrase therefore says she was unlucky in parents. This entire segment is a nominal clause without an explicit finite verb.
segments.s713-g20: 'اگر' introduces a conditional ('if/when'). 'تافتی' is an archaic/past-form of 'تافتن' used impersonally to mean 'shone' (here best read as 'when/if it shone'). 'بر سرش' is a prepositional phrase ('upon her head') where the enclitic 'ش' attaches to 'سر' as the 3rd-person possessive. 'آفتاب' is the noun governed by the verb 'تافتی'. Together the segment gives a time/condition subordinate to the previous nominal clause.
segments.s713-g21: 'چنان بد که' is a correlative phrasing meaning 'so (it was) that...' where 'چنان' ('so/thus') plus 'بد' (here adverbial 'badly/so') introduces a result clause. 'روزی' is a temporal adverb ('one day') placed before the manner/agent phrase 'پیاده به دشت' ('on foot into the plain') — the segment sets the time and manner of the ensuing action.
segments.s713-g22: 'همی' is the archaic/progressive particle often used before verbs to indicate a habitual or continuous action. 'گرد' here functions with 'بگشت' to mean 'to go about/roam around' (literally 'around that meadows he returned/turned'); 'آن مرغزاران' is the oblique locative 'those meadows' and 'بگشت' is the past-tense verb (he went/returned). Word order places the circumstantial modifier before the verb and the object after 'گرد'.
segments.s713-g23: A nominal title phrase: 'جهاندار خاقان' ('world-ruler, the khaghan') followed by the purposive prepositional phrase 'ز بهر شکار' ('for the sake of hunting'). 'ز' is the poetic/short form of 'from/for' and 'بهر' emphasizes purpose ('for/for the sake of'), so the whole segment names the subject and his purpose.
segments.s713-g24: 'بدشتی' is an adverbial locative ('to/on the plain'). 'دگر' ('other/another') qualifies the location; 'بود' is the past tense of 'to be' ('was'); 'زان' is a poetic preposition/particle meaning 'from/away from' or 'thereon/from that', and 'مرغزار' is 'meadow'. The segment therefore states that he was on a different plain, apart from that meadow.
segments.s713-g9: دو چنگش = 'his two claws' (دو numer + possessive noun with -ش). به کردار = a simile marker 'in the manner/form of' (به + کردار, where کردار here functions like 'manner'). چنگ هژبر = 'the claw of Hēžbar' (چنگ 'claw' governed by the following name/epithet هژبر). Word order is characteristic of classical Persian: modifiers follow the head noun.
segments.s713-g10: خروشش = 'his roar' (noun + possessive). همی‌برگذشتی is a classical/imperfective verbal form with همی- indicating continuous/ habitual aspect and -ی marking the old 3rd‑person ending: 'kept passing' or 'would pass'. ز ابر = 'from/over the clouds' (ز here ablative/agentive, meaning 'past/over the clouds'). The verb links back to خروشش as its subject.
segments.s713-g11: همی at the start gives a habitual/continuous sense ('would, kept on'). سنگ را is the definite object (سنگ 'stone' + را object marker). درکشیدی is the verb composed of در- + کشیدی (classical past/habitual sense) meaning 'drew/dragged/hauled' here. به دم is an adverbial phrase 'with a breath' or 'in one breath' (به + دم = by/measured by a breath).
segments.s713-g12: شده روز is a verbless/perfect construction where شده (become) qualifies روز (day) — 'the day became'. ازو = 'because of him' or 'from him'. بر بزرگان = 'upon the great ones' (بر + بزرگان). دژم is a predicate adjective 'grim/sullen' modifying the situation of the great ones. Word order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s713-g13: ورا is a directional/indirect object particle here ('to him' / 'thither'); شیر is used as a title 'lion' applied to a person. کپی in this line functions as a name or epithet attached to شیر (the precise lexical sense is archaic/poetic and probably a proper name), and همی‌خواندند is habitual past 'they used to call/sing/recite' (همی- for continuous aspect + خواندند past plural). Together: 'they used to call (him) "Shir Kapi".'
segments.s713-g14: ز رنجش = 'because of (his) anger' (ز = 'because of/from', رنجش = 'anger/resentment'). همه بوم = 'the whole land/realm' (همه 'all' + بوم 'homeland/realm'). در ماندند is a compound past 'remained inside' (در 'in' + ماندند 'they remained'). The clause says that, on account of his anger, the entire realm stayed indoors/was held back.
segments.s713-g15: یکی دختری = 'a certain daughter/a girl' (یکی 'a certain' + دختری 'a daughter/maiden'). داشت خاتون = 'the lady had' (داشت as past 'had', خاتون 'lady' is the possessor placed after the verb for poetic word order). چوماه is a simile 'like the moon' modifying the daughter. The line uses inverted/poetic order typical of classical Persian.
segments.s713-g16: اگر = 'if' (conditional). ماه = 'the moon' (used as the standard of beauty). دارد = present 'has/possesses'. دو زلف سیاه = 'two black locks/tresses' (دو 'two', زلف 'lock of hair', سیاه 'black'). The clause is a poetic assertion that, if the moon possesses any adornment, that adornment is two black tresses.

sentences.s713-s49: The lady said to him that not far from here there is a meadow, a beautiful place for a feast.
sentences.s713-s50: The young men of Chin are in that meadow; they hold a festival there in spring.
sentences.s713-s51: From that grove a shot, like an arrow, was hurled; you see a mountain darker than pitch.
sentences.s713-s52: Upon that flinty mountain there is a dragon, which makes this land of Chin be in calamity.
sentences.s713-s45: When the lady heard a sound behind the curtain, she grew alert and saw Bahram the hero.
sentences.s713-s46: He praised her profusely and cried 'Bravo!', saying, 'May Turk and China be prosperous for you.'
sentences.s713-s47: I will ask one wish of the king, that by that wish I may be made successful.
sentences.s713-s48: Bahram said to her, 'Your command is right; regarding this wish, your desire and your pledge stand.'
sentences.s713-s41: If that would be shameful — and if I were to speak my name — would my desire be fulfilled?
sentences.s713-s42: That long day also came; he kept the secret hidden from everyone.
sentences.s713-s43: So it happened that the Khaghan held a feast; at that brilliant banquet he made the world shine.
sentences.s713-s44: He sent for Bahram the hero; when he came, he set him on the golden throne.
sentences.s713-s61: When they saw his claws from afar, they rushed at his back, ear, head, and mane.
sentences.s713-s62: It cried out, piercing the heart of the warrior; as for me — whether (it was) lion, elephant, or whale.
sentences.s713-s63: No one would come before him; whether his number was few or many.
sentences.s713-s64: To him Bahram said: 'Tomorrow at dawn I will come and see this place of festivity.'
sentences.s713-s53: One called it "Kapish the lion"; another likewise did not know its name.
sentences.s713-s54: One of my daughters (was) from the Khagan of China; you made her like the sun — bravo!
sentences.s713-s55: He came from the iwan to that place of celebration, for the Khagan had gone out hunting with his army.
sentences.s713-s56: A grim dragon came down from the mountain; he took that spring from me — he was my ruin.
sentences.s713-s65: By the power of Yazdān, which He gave as lofty might — the High Creator of the moon and Vahūr.
sentences.s713-s66: I will wrest the festival-place from the dragon when the night‑visitors show us the way.
sentences.s713-s37: If they say my daughter was taken by that beast (the lion-kapi), my jewel (my honour) will be disgraced.
sentences.s713-s38: They do not know that the grim dragon can seize a mountain of iron with its breath.
sentences.s713-s39: If the king’s daughter were renowned, that very king’s life would be dear (i.e. precious to him).
sentences.s713-s40: The lady said to him: ‘I will take my vengeance for (the sake of) my world‑seer/kinsman.’
sentences.s713-s57: Now, every spring it likewise comes to that meadow for hunting.
sentences.s713-s58: In this city no youth of ours remained; that famed champion (or famed heroism) remained not as well.
sentences.s713-s59: They were destroyed in pursuit of the young lion; the dust of the prosperous homeland was raised up.
sentences.s713-s60: Many Chinese horsemen and men of deeds charged into that mountain country.
sentences.s713-s29: The lady said to him that, by his farr, it is fitting that we take pride in his plumage.
sentences.s713-s30: I will ask him one proper request: if the khāqān does not undertake it, he is unfit for the task.
sentences.s713-s31: Perhaps he will seek from the dragon vengeance against me; go that he may hear my pain and my curse.
sentences.s713-s32: He said to him, 'O detractor, (let him) read this tale; go to Chief Rāstān.'
sentences.s713-s25: Once upon a time in Iran there was a king; his crown was loftier than the moon.
sentences.s713-s26: His great men called him Bahram‑Gerd, for he bore a name among the kings.
sentences.s713-s27: Now — until he comes from Iran, assemble — the earth itself trembles beneath his steed.
sentences.s713-s28: They called his chief 'Khoda‑vand', and they set the royal crown upon his head.
sentences.s713-s1: After a while that era came, and night and day brought peace and ease.
sentences.s713-s2: So bad that in Mount Chin at that time beasts and snares were more numerous than one could imagine.
sentences.s713-s3: There was a beast larger in body than a horse; its fallen mane was like musk, its tresses like a rein.
sentences.s713-s4: His body was yellow and his ears and mouth black; no one saw him except in the warm place.
sentences.s713-s13: That same lady, too, in the inner palace took counsel and, with a certain guide, gave her decision.
sentences.s713-s14: When that lion Kapi saw him from his mountain, he came down and dragged him by the tail to the gate.
sentences.s713-s15: In a single moment he was hidden from the world; he became foremost among the world's fair‑faced ones.
sentences.s713-s16: When the khāqān heard that, he made his face dark; that same mother too tore at her hair.
sentences.s713-s33: You will find no trace of the lion's imitation, unless it has been slain and the wolf drags its paw.
sentences.s713-s34: When the lady heard these words she became glad; the girl was freed from her care.
sentences.s713-s35: He rode on until he came before the khaghan; one by one he told (them) what he had seen and heard.
sentences.s713-s36: The khaghan said to him that it was free from reproach (or permissible) to be in a place where a horseman like me was.
sentences.s713-s21: It so happened that one day a horseman from Iran — one of a hundred renowned men — saw him.
sentences.s713-s22: Bahram, with a guide, was driving many foot soldiers forward into the inner court.
sentences.s713-s23: The lady asked who this man was, for he had high rank and divine glory.
sentences.s713-s24: She said to him, “You lesser one, keep away from desire; for Bahram is a hero called Rândâni.”
sentences.s713-s17: Because of her pain they wept year after year; as if they were roasted on a fierce fire.
sentences.s713-s18: They kept seeking a remedy for that dragon — asking until when Chin would be freed from its grip.
sentences.s713-s19: When Bahram fought a fierce battle, from that he raised a warlike man.
sentences.s713-s20: The lady would go to visit him; to everyone she recounted his deeds.
sentences.s713-s9: Two red lips and a nose like the point of a pen; two (cheeks) smiling and a sullen narcissus (eye).
sentences.s713-s10: That trembling maiden was unfortunate in mother and father; if the sun shone upon her head...
sentences.s713-s11: It was so that, one day, on foot into the plain he went about those meadows.
sentences.s713-s12: The world-ruler, the khaghan, for the sake of hunting was on another plain, away from that meadow.
sentences.s713-s5: His two claws were like Hēžbar’s claw; his roar kept passing above the clouds.
sentences.s713-s6: He could haul (or lift) a stone in a single breath; because of him the day became grim for the great ones.
sentences.s713-s7: They used to call to him 'Shir Kapi' (the lion Kapi); because of his anger the whole land stayed indoors.
sentences.s713-s8: A lady had a daughter like the moon; if the moon has anything, it is two black tresses.
